author: @Wuety06
text:
Have u put a DMM set2 resistance (b4 and after) ? Why is so little apparently known about this? .  My initial search turned up one relevant paper published by dudeat Nasa in 1974.  Does it have a non immediately obvious limitation or flaw?  Is the adhesion method known? I haven't read that paper yet but I'm free associating it to be like friction stir welding?

 I'm building a Resistive spot welder/cutter  (cap discharge variety with 111f of Maxwells 6s2p) electrod clamps /cable attach point is  aluminum.  I own a rock tumbler if I drive am hr to my parents if you haven't tested it and would like the info I'll let u know if I try it.  

This looks like it could be the ticket to even lower resistance to the weld point.  Especially with short pulses bringing skin effect into play.  Any idea if it could be done with silver?     

Great video as always!

Random last question but would u happen to know of any esoteric substances to add to a copper 2 chloride etch bath to reduce undercut or somehow better the bath? I've got the right color and it works as it should in my aerated tank. I just keep reading comments that industry knows a few more tricks and I'm always looking for a quality improvement but making it was the first time I've used Chem outside of one lab at  university. I consulted with a friend  who has a strong background to avoid gassing the neighbors.  :D

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: peen plating is a standard industry practice mate - i found loads on it. As for the second question i am afraid not I really don't know that much about electroplating i am afraid

replies.author: @Wuety06
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith : I found more information later with the "ee" spelling.   in the video I hear/believe u spelled it  "pean" but I noticed a heasitation on 2nd viewing.  Spelling never been your strong suit? Usually isn't mine. Nor is word for word comprehension in an echo prone environment lol. At the moment I'm removing metal (etch) to make circuit boards but I do plan to plate holes between layers (vias) instead of rivets. Powdered Graphite in nail Polish/varnish is supposed to work as a hole activator  and  using my favorite health tonic of H2SO4 + CuSO4•5H2O  + HCL with some dc current I should be able to plate

 I use cupric chloride for etching...Made that  tank really cheap by using acetone to weld plexi. 

  Is the video where u make the Nanoparticle solution with a coper salt and ascorbic the same particles you used here?  thanks for the reply above. I hope all is going well for you!

author: @roidroid
text:
Could be a way of coating aluminium PCBs with a thin layer of copper to make soldering easier.  It'd use less copper than traditional fully-copper PCB traces.  (i worry about a "peak copper" future)

Does it only coat metals, or would it coat plastics too?  I ask because the aluminium PCBs i experiment with have a scotch-tape backing and there's no reason to waste copper on that, so it'd be good if it resists it.

If it doesn't resist it - well there's applications for that too (an interesting way of making flexible copper-trace PCBs).

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's well worth a try mate

replies.author: @roidroid
replies.text:
Cheers Rob.   I ask incase someone in the community already has experience with it, and might be able to save someone else the wasted time from needlessly repeating a dead-end experiment.
Pooling our collective knowledge is a good time saver.

author: @davidlarson3905
text:
[Restoration/Fabrication artist here] I use a similar method of "plating" with brass and copper for giving certain surfaces different patinas on old engines and sculptures. It works quite well! I've found that it works much better with brass if you heat the metal to be "plated" prior to peening. Although, I suppose in a scenario like this, extra heat might be unnecessary and inefficient waste.

Great video once again!

-David

author: @greygleam
text: Nice idea, but... Unless the coat is absolutely free of defects you are prone to experience some accelerated corrosion on the Aluminium.  It would be OK if kept dry but Amy moisture that can act as an electrolyte will form a galvanic element at the defects, with copper as the more noble of the two the Aluminium is the one to erode. Also known as galvanic corrosion.  With zinc on steel for example the less noble(zinc) is the top coat and the one to dissolve thus protecting the steel.  Decorative: yes ;functional :???  I am having doubts... But still a nice idea by overall concept.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Mate - read around on peen plating - this isn't my idea it is a kitchen adaptation of an industrial technique

replies.author: @greygleam
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith I am not questioning the feasibility of the peen plating process, it's indeed used industrially.  It's the combination of a copper coat on Aluminium.  I don't know what your uses are so you may be fine- but as said any moisture  or wet environment with ionic content(distilled water would do nothing) is prone to cause corrosion defects in the Aluminium at spots not 100% covered.  It's simple electrochemistry.  Put it in a closed container with some salty water for a week...

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: ok thanks for the input - but the point was peen coating - i chose copper only because it is traditionally hard to coat onto Ali - no other reson

author: @BluesDoctor
text: The horrid Tape Peel Test, lol.... In years of PVD, Physical Vapor Deposition work, we always had some anxiety with a new coating and the QA Tape Peel Test. Plenty to go wrong that will cause the test to fail, my job was to go figure out why it failed. The nano-metal particle peening reminded me of some explosives clad work early in my career. I can't even remember what my job was in all of that, just the cheap thrill watching the explosives expert who ran the work and did it all day, day after day for a month or more. Of course I checked out the physics of all that at the time and thinking this is pretty interesting stuff, expect there are some common threads in this. I will plan to spend a little time revisiting those ideas and see what comes of it. Cheers
